dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 5 responding for chest pain at Grace Street.
Hi, I'm Donna, an EMT with the rescue squad. May I ask what brought you to call 911 today?
Yes, chest pain. It came on suddenly after I was assaulted a few minutes ago.
Can you tell me your name, please?
Yes, it's Marilyn.
Okay, Marilyn, can you tell me what county we're currently in?
Richmond County.
What is today's date?
July 8th, 2020.
And what happened to you today?
Someone attacked me, and I took some drugs.
Marilyn, can you take a deep breath for me?
She is breathing evenly with no signs of distress.
Radial pulse is strong and rapid. Skin is pink, warm, and dry.
Marilyn, can you describe the chest pain? 16. History of Present Illness; S.A.M.P.L.E. : signs and symptoms; Patient: It feels sharp and is located on the left side of my chest.
Does it radiate anywhere? 18. History of Present Illness; S.A.M.P.L.E. : signs and symptoms; Patient: Yeah, maybe to my right arm.
When did the pain start? 20. History of Present Illness; S.A.M.P.L.E. : signs and symptoms; Patient: Around 5 PM.
Is the pain reproducible with palpation? 22. History of Present Illness; S.A.M.P.L.E. : signs and symptoms; Patient: Yes, pressing there hurts.
Have you had any other symptoms, like cough, fever, or sweating? 24. History of Present Illness; S.A.M.P.L.E. : signs and symptoms; Patient: No, just the chest pain and a little anxiety.
Marilyn, do you have any allergies?
No, I don't.
What medications are you currently taking?
Citalopram, and I just took two baby aspirin.
Have you had any past medical issues?
I have bipolar disorder and anxiety.
Have you had any issues with substance use?
I used to take cocaine. I was clean for six months until today.
When was your last meal or drink?
I'm not sure. 35. History of Present Illness; S.A.M.P.L.E. : events leading up to the event; EMT: Can you tell me what happened before this injury?

dialogue.micro_intent: events leading up to the event
dialogue.utterance: I was using cocaine with someone when he became violent.

dialogue.role: events leading up to the event; EMT
dialogue.utterance: Was this the same person who assaulted you?

dialogue.micro_intent: events leading up to the event
dialogue.utterance: Yes, that's the one.
How did the pain first come on?
All of a sudden after the assault.
Does anything make the pain worse? 42. Pain Assessment; O.P.Q.R.S.T. : provocation; Patient: Yes, pressing on my chest.
Would you describe the pain as sharp, burning, throbbing, or something else?
Definitely sharp. 45. Pain Assessment; O.P.Q.R.S.T. : severity; EMT: On a scale of 0 to 10, how would you rate the pain? 46. Pain Assessment; O.P.Q.R.S.T. : severity; Patient: It's about a 7.
When did you last feel the same level of pain? 48. Pain Assessment; O.P.Q.R.S.T. : time; Patient: Just a few minutes ago.
Bruising is noted on both wrists and a minor abrasion on the right knee.
Any rashes or unusual skin texture?
No, just the bruises.
Marilyn, are you experiencing any dizziness or headaches?
No, I'm not.
Have you had any nausea or vomiting?
No, none of those either.
There is slight chest tenderness on the left side. Any pain when breathing?
No, just pain with pressure.
Pulse is 120 per minute.
Respirations are even at 18 per minute.
Blood pressure is 121/84.
SpO₂ is 97% on room air.
We've got a glucose reading of 0 here-just a note, we'll proceed without.
Patient took 2 baby aspirin before we arrived. No further medication is required at this point.
We're applying 4-lead monitoring now to start tracking your vitals.
No changes from the first EKG, so we'll keep 4-lead monitoring.
12-lead EKG is ready - results show sinus tachycardia and minor T-wave changes, but no major issues.
The findings align with a cardiac suspected protocol due to rapid heart rate and chest trauma.
We'll follow medical chest pain - cardiac suspected protocol.
We've documented that you are A&O x4 with a GCS of 15.
Marilyn, would you like to be transported to VCU Health for further evaluation?
No. I don't feel like I need facilitated transport right now.
We understand, but would you like a copy of your EKG and vital numbers for your friend to take with you?
Yes, that would be great.
Your friend will come get you and take you to VCU for continued care, right?
That's correct, yes.
We'll provide them with a copy of your EKG and all notes so you can be seen quickly.
We've also added you into the system so you can reach us again if needed.
Since you were A&O x4 with no neurological deficits, your current decision reflects your ability to remain in control.
We'll call ahead so the ER should be ready to see you earlier.
Is there anything else we can do before your friend arrives?
No, I think that will be all.
Let me just check in - does the pain still feel like a 7 right now?
It's the same as it was earlier.
Just to reconfirm, you have no history of previous heart problems?
No, I've never had any.
Marilyn, would you mind telling me about your mental health more?
I've been on Citalopram for years for my bipolar and anxiety. It helps.
Okay, thank you for that. Any other thoughts you'd like us to consider before your friend arrives?
No. I'm going to get evaluated, just not with your help.
Your friend will be here soon. If there's any worsening of symptoms, we expect you to call 911 again.
Yes, I understand.
Please ask the hospital to confirm the bruising on your left chest and wrist if they don't notice it.
I'll make sure to mention it.
Final pulse check - still 120. No significant changes.
SpO₂ still reads 97%. Skin remains pink and dry.
12-lead readings stable. No new abnormalities detected.
Mental status remains A&O x4. No hallucinations or confusion.
All procedures documented. Handoff communication to ER is active.
Any additional concerns before your friend gets here?
No, everything seems in order.
From the scene to VCU by private car. Be safe, Marilyn. Call us if you need help.
I will, thanks for the help.
